post: English judges had lifetime appointments since 1701. Colonial judges served at the king's pleasure. That gap is Grievance 8 of the Declaration of Independence, and it's more than a procedural complaint. The Act of Settlement (1701) established that judges in England would be appointed for "good behavior," insulating them from political pressure and ensuring they could protect subjects' rights without fear of removal. Colonists wanted the same protection. Several colonies attempted to pass laws establishing identical terms for their own judges. The king consistently blocked them. By 1761, he had made the policy explicit: colonial judges would serve at the pleasure of the Crown. For Jefferson, this wasn't a legal technicality. It was evidence that the king was applying two different standards of English rights depending on where you lived, and that colonists were deliberately receiving less protection under the law than subjects in England. The principle mattered enough that it survived the Revolution entirely. Article III of the U. S. Constitution specifies that appointed federal judges hold their offices during good behavior, the exact protection the king spent decades denying the colonies. Chris Canty of George Washington University breaks it down in Episode 8 of our 27-part series. Discover more and join us for Constitution Day Live →  https://bit.ly/42cJB0M New episodes every Monday, Wednesday, and Friday.  #DeclarationOfIndependence  #AmericanHistory  #CivicsEducation  #HistoryTeachers  #SeparationOfPowers

In 1763, the British drew a line at the Appalachian Mountains and told colonists they couldn't cross it. By 1776, the colonies were crowded. People believed land to the west meant better lives, for themselves and their families. But the Proclamation Line held. Meanwhile, immigration restrictions made it harder for new settlers to naturalize and join the colonial economy at all. Grievance 7 of the Declaration of Independence is about population control on paper. But as Jennifer Seiter of the UVA Democracy and Capitalism Lab explains, the colonists read it as something larger: a British government actively limiting not just their present circumstances, but their prospects for the future. For Jefferson, that was reason enough to declare independence. Government has one essential job: protect the people. When King George III dissolved colonial governments, he didn't just remove leadership, he left the colonies without any legal protection from invasion or internal instability. Jefferson called it near-anarchy. And for a generation steeped in Enlightenment thinking, that failure had a name: a broken social contract. Locke, Rousseau, and others had articulated the idea clearly: the people agree to be governed, the government agrees to protect them. When the government stops holding up its end, the people are no longer bound by theirs. Grievance 6 is Jefferson making that argument in plain terms. The king had dissolved his own legitimacy. What came next was the right to start over. Dr. Matthew Goetz of White House Historical Association explains in Episode 6 of our 27-part series. "To demote politics is to elevate the limitless opportunity of every human person. Big congratulations to Megan Thompson, Minnesota civics teacher, AP Gov instructor, and winner of the Civic Gold Star Award in the Civic Star Challenge 🏆 When her students needed to make sense of civil unrest happening in their own city, Megan turned to primary sources, founding principles, and the kind of teaching that lets students find their own answers. BRI and iCivics are proud to also recognize 20 Silver Star and 50 Bronze Star winners from classrooms across all 50 states. The Declaration of Independence is alive in America's classrooms this year, and teachers like Megan are why. There's a difference between a government you disagree with and a government that eliminates your voice entirely. The colonists understood that distinction clearly by the 1770s. When colonial assemblies pushed back against British taxation, King George III didn't negotiate, he dissolved them. Massachusetts first, then North Carolina and Virginia, then any colony that moved toward organizing a Continental Congress. Grievance 5 of the Declaration of Independence marks a turning point: the moment colonists stopped seeing British policy as overreach and started seeing it as tyranny. The shutdown of representative government wasn't a political disagreement anymore. It was a crisis of sovereignty. Episode 5 of our 27-part series is live. If the danger was going to come to our political institutions it would come from within.
